sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Tyler, a 56-year-old male, presents with a 4-5 week history of difficulty swallowing, feeling like something is stuck in his throat, which is painful and occurs when he tries to eat solid foods.
He denies coughing, vomiting, or blood in his stool.
He reports no weight loss or changes in his weight.
Past medical history: Diabetes and high blood pressure, currently on metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes and norvasc for blood pressure.
His last hemoglobin A1c was 7.4.
Review of systems: Denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or any other side effects from medications.
He reports that his blood sugars are within target range, checked about 80% of the time.
Current medications: Metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes, norvasc for high blood pressure.
Objective:
Vital signs: BP 120/80 mmHg, not mentioned for other vital signs.
Physical exam: The neck is fine, no swelling or masses felt in the neck or thyroid.
The throat looks okay, no masses or swelling, no redness, no carotid bruit.
The lung exam is clear, heart exam is normal, no murmurs.
The belly exam shows epigastric tenderness, but no masses or significant swelling.
The back exam is normal, and the extremity and neurological exams are fine.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has difficulty swallowing due to a suspected narrowing of the esophagus, possibly a stricture.
Problem list: Difficulty swallowing (suspected stricture); diabetes (well-controlled); high blood pressure (well-controlled).
Plan:
I will refer Tyler to gastroenterology for a scope to evaluate the esophagus.
I will start him on prilosec 20mg once a day for reflux.
I will also recommend that he chew his foods frequently, especially hard foods like steak or meat, to prevent getting them stuck.
I will order another hemoglobin A1c and some blood work to check kidney function.
I will also give him an eye referral to ophthalmology to check for diabetic retinopathy.
I will continue the metformin and norvasc as is.
I will have him return in a month to review the results of the blood tests and adjust his treatment as needed.
Follow-up: I will have Tyler return in a month to review the results of the blood tests and adjust his treatment as needed.
